skill.name: wezterm
skill.description: Control WezTerm terminal sessions through the WezTerm CLI, including panes, tabs, workspaces, domains, and text sent to panes.
skill.summary: Manage WezTerm panes, tabs, workspaces, domains, and terminal input from AI coding tools.
skill.author: Dicklesworthstone

security_audit.summary: The Ruby backtick findings are false positives caused by Markdown inline code and code fences, not Ruby execution. The skill still intentionally guides an assistant to run WezTerm CLI commands, spawn terminal processes, connect domains, and send text into panes, so misuse could execute commands in an active user terminal. No prompt injection, credential theft, obfuscation, or malicious network exfiltration was found in SKILL.md.

medium_findings.title: Terminal Command Execution Capability
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for operational risk: the skill documents commands that can spawn programs and start new terminal sessions. This is legitimate WezTerm usage, but it can execute arbitrary commands when a user or assistant supplies the command after the separator.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documented WezTerm commands explicitly launch terminal programs. The context is a terminal-control skill, so the behavior is expected but still security-sensitive.

medium_findings.title: Text Injection Into Active Terminal Panes
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for operational risk: the skill shows how to send text into a WezTerm pane. If used without confirmation, this can run commands in an already authenticated shell or affect a remote session.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The send-text examples clearly target terminal panes. The security impact depends on user confirmation and the active shell context.

medium_findings.title: Remote Domain Session Control
medium_findings.description: TRUE POSITIVE for contextual risk: the skill includes examples for spawning sessions in SSH domains and connecting to a mux server. This is normal WezTerm functionality, but it can interact with remote systems if the user has configured them.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples reference SSH domains and mux connections. No malicious endpoint is present, but the feature crosses local terminal boundaries.

low_findings.title: Markdown Backtick Static Analysis False Positives
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: the repeated Ruby or shell backtick findings refer to Markdown inline code and fenced command examples. SKILL.md contains documentation, not Ruby code that executes backticks.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file is Markdown and the flagged syntax appears inside documentation examples. There is no Ruby runtime file or executable wrapper in the analyzed file.

low_findings.title: Documented Hidden Configuration Path
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious filesystem access: the hidden path is the normal WezTerm configuration file location. The skill documents the path but does not read, write, or exfiltrate the file.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line only names a standard configuration path. No command in the file modifies or uploads that path.

low_findings.title: Benign System Reconnaissance Examples
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE for malicious reconnaissance: pane activation and a simple directory listing example are normal terminal workflow examples. They can reveal local state if executed, but no collection or exfiltration behavior is shown.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are ordinary terminal operations in a terminal-control skill. The residual risk is user-context exposure, not confirmed malicious reconnaissance.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm False Positive
low_findings.description: FALSE POSITIVE: the flagged description line contains no cryptographic implementation, hash selection, or encryption instruction. No evidence found for weak cryptography in SKILL.md.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited line is plain frontmatter text describing WezTerm control. It does not reference MD5, SHA1, ciphers, or cryptographic behavior.

dangerous_patterns.title: Command Launch Through WezTerm CLI
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill documents WezTerm commands that can launch user-specified programs. Require explicit user approval before running commands that spawn processes or send text to panes.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is directly documented and is inherent to the skill purpose. It is risky when automation executes commands without user review.

content.user_title: Control WezTerm Sessions from AI Assistants
content.value_statement: Terminal multiplexing often needs precise pane, tab, and workspace control. This skill gives Claude, Codex, and Claude Code concise WezTerm CLI patterns for managing active terminal sessions.
actual_capabilities: List WezTerm panes, clients, workspaces, and domains through documented CLI commands.
Create and activate panes or tabs, including horizontal and vertical splits.
Send text to current or selected WezTerm panes for interactive terminal workflows.
Start new WezTerm windows and launch commands in new terminal contexts.
Reference the standard WezTerm configuration path and show effective key bindings.
limitations: It requires WezTerm to be installed and available by path or PATH lookup.
It does not validate commands before they are sent to a terminal pane.
It assumes the user has already configured SSH domains or mux servers.
It provides command references, not a dedicated wrapper script or API client.

use_cases.title: Organize Coding Sessions
use_cases.description: Create panes for an editor, tests, logs, and shell commands inside one WezTerm workspace.

use_cases.title: Manage Remote Terminal Domains
use_cases.description: List domains and open sessions for configured SSH targets from an AI-assisted workflow.

use_cases.title: Automate Terminal Layout Checks
use_cases.description: Inspect current panes, activate specific targets, and send reviewed commands to the right terminal.

prompt_templates.title: List My WezTerm Panes
prompt_templates.prompt: Show me the WezTerm command to list all panes, then explain what each field means.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Development Layout
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me create a WezTerm layout with one editor pane, one test pane, and one log pane.

prompt_templates.title: Target a Specific Pane
prompt_templates.prompt: Use WezTerm CLI patterns to identify a pane and prepare a safe command for that pane. Ask before sending text.

prompt_templates.title: Coordinate Workspaces and Domains
prompt_templates.prompt: Plan a WezTerm workflow that lists workspaces, checks domains, and opens an SSH domain session only after confirmation.

output: Use the WezTerm pane listing command.
Review the pane identifiers, workspace names, and domain names.
Choose the target pane before sending any terminal input.

output_examples.input: Set up a two-pane development view.
output: Create a horizontal or vertical split based on the current layout.
Open the editor in one pane and reserve the second pane for tests.
Select the configured SSH domain by name.
Ask for confirmation before opening the remote session.

best_practices: Ask for confirmation before spawning commands or sending text into a pane.
List panes and domains before targeting a specific terminal session.
Prefer explicit pane identifiers when more than one session is active.
anti_patterns: Do not send shell text to an active pane without showing the user first.
Do not assume an SSH domain name exists without listing configured domains.
Do not modify the WezTerm configuration file unless the user asks for that change.

faq.question: Does this skill install WezTerm?
faq.answer: No. It assumes WezTerm is already installed and reachable from the system path or application path.

faq.question: Can it manage tabs and panes?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents commands for listing, splitting, spawning, activating, and zooming panes or tabs.

faq.question: Can it send commands to a terminal?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes send-text examples, so users should confirm commands before execution.

faq.question: Does it support remote sessions?
faq.answer: It references WezTerm SSH domains and mux connections when those are already configured by the user.

faq.question: Does it read my WezTerm config?
faq.answer: No. It only documents the standard configuration path and a command to show effective keys.

faq.question: Is this safe for marketplace publication?
faq.answer: Yes, with a medium-risk warning because it can control active terminals and launch commands.
